timeline.label: FY2025: $3.42B capex and $496M startup costs on Kentucky plate, West Virginia sheet, and Arizona melt shop; three-year capex ~$8.9B
FY2025 record steel-mill shipments 25.3M tons at 83% utilization; segment pretax $2.38B mills / $1.23B products
Balance sheet: $2.70B cash/investments, $7.12B debt/leases, undrawn $2.25B revolver; funded debt to capital 24.4%
West Virginia sheet mill disclosed at ~$3.65B net for ~3M t annual capacity; expected completion by year-end 2026
2026 capex guidance $2.5B as major projects near completion
Prior year steel-mill utilization 76% versus 83% in 2025; cyclical rebound in shipments and spreads
Leon Topalian continues as CEO; incentive pay tied to ROE, safety, and strategic project execution (DEF 14A cycle)
Multi-year growth program: new sheet, plate, rebar, towers, and coating capacity announced across U.S. footprint
Nucor maintains dividend through prior steel downturn; balance-sheet conservatism versus integrated peers
Section 232 tariffs reshape U.S. steel trade; Nucor benefits as domestic EAF leader while import risks persist

industry.structure: U.S. steel is concentrated among Nucor, Cleveland-Cliffs, U.S. Steel (now Cliffs-owned), and Steel Dynamics in flat and long products, with Nucor the largest EAF producer. Pricing follows global commodity spreads, scrap/DRI input costs, and import arbitrage; no single producer sets price.
industry.share_shift: EAF share of U.S. production continues to rise versus integrated blast-furnace capacity; Nucor gains on cost position and downstream integration (fabrication, rebar, joist, deck) while new domestic sheet capacity from multiple players increases supply-response risk.
industry.trend: Infrastructure and reshoring support domestic demand, but 2024–2026 brings a wave of new sheet and plate capacity, high startup costs, and volatile scrap/spread economics; AI/data-center and energy project demand partially offsets traditional construction cycles.
